senses.definition: A team; a group of people who work together professionally.

subsenses.definition: A group of individuals who work together for a common purpose.
(military) A unit of approximately sixty to one hundred and twenty soldiers, typically consisting of two or three platoons and forming part of a battalion.

quotes.text: It was by his order the shattered leading company flung itself into the houses when the Sin Verguenza were met by an enfilading volley as they reeled into the calle.
quotes.reference: 1907 January, Harold Bindloss, chapter 30, in The Dust of Conflict, 1st Canadian edition, Toronto, Ont.: McLeod & Allen, →OCLC:

subsenses.definition: A unit of firefighters and their equipment.
examples: It took six companies to put out the fire.

subsenses.definition: (nautical) The entire crew of a ship.

examples: As he had worked for the CIA for over 30 years, he would soon take retirement from the company.

senses.definition: A small group of birds or animals.
(law) An entity having legal personality, and thus able to own property and to sue and be sued in its own name; a corporation.

quotes.text: “[…] That woman is stark mad, Lord Stranleigh.[…]If she had her way, she’d ruin the company inside a year with her hare-brained schemes; love of the people, and that sort of guff.”
quotes.reference: 1913, Robert Barr, chapter 4, in Lord Stranleigh Abroad:

senses.definition: (business) Any business, whether incorporated or not, that manufactures or sells products (also known as goods), or provides services as a commercial venture.
examples: Marconi set up the company who made the first radios.

quotes.text: In order to grant the rich these pleasures, the social contract is reconfigured. […] The public realm is privatised, the regulations restraining the ultra-wealthy and the companies they control are abandoned, and Edwardian levels of inequality are almost fetishised.
quotes.reference: 2013 May 17, George Monbiot, “Money just makes the rich suffer”, in The Guardian Weekly, volume 188, number 23, page 19:

quotes.text: According to this saga of intellectual-property misanthropy, these creatures [patent trolls] roam the business world, buying up patents and then using them to demand extravagant payouts from companies they accuse of infringing them. Often, their victims pay up rather than face the costs of a legal battle.
quotes.reference: 2013 June 8, “Obama goes troll-hunting”, in The Economist, volume 407, number 8839, page 95:

quotes.text: The company added it has not “received any reports of adverse events,” but it’s moving forward with the recall out of “an abundance of caution.”
quotes.reference: 2021 November 26, Jackie Wattles, “Some Old Spice and Secret deodorants recalled after cancer-causing chemical is detected”, in CNN Business:

examples: Keep the house clean; I have company coming.

quotes.text: Come, O thou Traveller unknown, / Whom still I hold, but cannot see! / My company before is gone, / And I am left alone with Thee; / With Thee all night I mean to stay, / And wrestle till the break of day.
quotes.reference: 1742, “Come, O Thou Traveler Unknown”, Charles Wesley (music):

quotes.text: At length, one night, when the company by ſome accident broke up much ſooner than ordinary, ſo that the candles were not half burnt out, ſhe was not able to reſiſt the temptation, but reſolved to have them ſome way or other. Accordingly, as ſoon as the hurry was over, and the ſervants, as ſhe thought, all gone to ſleep, ſhe ſtole out of her bed, and went down ſtairs, naked to her ſhift as ſhe was, with a deſign to ſteal them […]
quotes.reference: 1762, Charles Johnstone, The Reverie; or, A Flight to the Paradise of Fools, volume 2, Dublin: Printed by Dillon Chamberlaine, →OCLC, page 202:

quotes.text: The departure was not unduly prolonged. In the road Mr. Love and the driver favoured the company with a brief chanty running. “Got it?—No, I ain't, 'old on,—Got it? Got it?—No, 'old on sir.”
quotes.reference: 1921, Ben Travers, chapter 5, in A Cuckoo in the Nest, Garden City, N.Y.: Doubleday, Page & Company, published 1925, →OCLC:

senses.definition: (A group of) adversaries, enemies, or rivals; unwanted company.

quotes.text: He used to drop into my chambers once in a while to smoke, and was first-rate company. When I gave a dinner there was generally a cover laid for him. I liked the man for his own sake, and even had he promised to turn out a celebrity it would have had no weight with me.
quotes.reference: 1897 December (indicated as 1898), Winston Churchill, chapter I, in The Celebrity: An Episode, New York, N.Y.: The Macmillan Company; London: Macmillan & Co., Ltd., →OCLC, page 2:

quotes.text: So I go into the darkness of the night / All alone I walk the streets until I find / Someone who is just like me / Looking for some company
quotes.reference: 1972, The Stylistics, “Children of The Night”, in Round 2:

senses.definition: (archaic, transitive) To accompany, keep company with.

quotes.text: Ye dooe knowe howe thatt hytt ys an vnlawefull thynge for a man beynge a iewe to company or come vnto an alient […].
quotes.reference: 1526, [William Tyndale, transl.], The Newe Testamẽt […] (Tyndale Bible), [Worms, Germany: Peter Schöffer], →OCLC, Acts x:[28], folio clxix, recto:

quotes.text: it was with a distinctly fallen countenance that his father hearkened to his mother's parenthetical request to “’bide hyar an’ company leetle Moses whilst I be a-milkin’ the cow.”
quotes.reference: 1891, Mary Noailles Murfree, In the "Stranger People's" Country, Nebraska, published 2005, page 2:

quotes.text: Men which have companied with us all the time.
quotes.reference: 1611, The Holy Bible, […] (King James Version), London: […] Robert Barker, […], →OCLC, Acts 1:21:

senses.definition: (obsolete, intransitive) To be a lively, cheerful companion.

quotes.text:
If thee list unto the Court to throng
[…]there thou needs must learne, to laugh, to lie,
To face, to forge, to scoffe, to companie.
quotes.reference: 1591, Ed[mund] Sp[enser], “Prosopopoia. Or Mother Hubberds Tale.”, in Complaints. Containing Sundrie Small Poemes of the Worlds Vanitie. […], London: […] William Ponsonbie, […], →OCLC:

quotes.text: companying with Infidels may not be simply condemned
quotes.reference: a. 1656, Joseph Hall, Epistle to Mr. I. F.:
